discharge_readiness_rationale: Physician agent has marked discharge_ready: false due to unresolved disposition conflict, incomplete goals-of-care conversation, and multiple critical transition elements not yet confirmed; discharge cannot proceed until these are resolved.

lace_score.implication: 30-day readmission risk approximately 18–22%. Follow-up within 48–72 hours is indicated. Home health should be strongly considered. Given patient is refusing IRF and has only 20 outpatient PT visits available for calendar year 2026, aggressive post-discharge support structures are essential to prevent readmission.

conflicts.summary: Three-way conflict between clinical recommendation for IRF, patient refusal of any inpatient facility, and inadequate outpatient PT coverage under Medicare Advantage

positions.holder: PT Agent / Physician Agent / Clinical Team
positions.position: Acute Inpatient Rehabilitation Facility (IRF) is clinically indicated — patient meets all IRF criteria, evidence-based outcomes for MCA stroke at this severity are significantly superior to home PT, and the 20-visit outpatient cap is structurally insufficient for meaningful motor recovery.
Refuses any inpatient facility. States: 'I am not going to a nursing home.' Wife supports patient's decision.

IRF is covered (20 days, qualifying stay met). However, outpatient PT/OT is capped at 20 combined visits for all of calendar year 2026 — insufficient for MCA stroke rehabilitation if home discharge proceeds.

resolution_options.option: Patient accepts IRF after goals-of-care conversation clarifies IRF is not a nursing home and explains functional consequences of refusal
resolution_options.trade_off: Optimal clinical outcome; patient must overcome stated objection. Requires time and skilled counseling.

resolution_options.option: Patient maintains refusal; proceed with home discharge with maximum available supports
resolution_options.trade_off: Respects patient autonomy but accepts significantly elevated risk of permanent left arm functional deficit, fall-related injury, and 30-day readmission. 20 outpatient visits will be exhausted in 6–7 weeks.

resolution_options.option: Explore home health PT/OT eligibility under Medicare Advantage home health benefit (separate from outpatient cap) to supplement inadequate outpatient coverage
resolution_options.trade_off: If patient meets homebound criteria, this could significantly extend available rehabilitation. Eligibility not yet assessed — requires urgent verification.

resolution_options.option: Appeal to Medicare Advantage for visit limit exception based on acute stroke medical necessity
resolution_options.trade_off: May unlock additional visits but process takes time and approval is not guaranteed. Should be initiated regardless of other decisions.

resolution_options.option: Short-term SNF with intensive PT/OT as middle-ground between IRF and home
resolution_options.trade_off: Lower intensity than IRF but more structure than home. May trigger same 'nursing home' objection from patient. Coverage requires verification.

conflicts.summary: Driving restriction not yet communicated to patient who has history of independent driving

positions.position: Patient must not drive until cleared by neurology — Massachusetts guidance restricts driving post-stroke typically 3–6 months. Left hemiparesis, proprioceptive deficits, and dysmetria create unacceptable risk.
Former firefighter, active driver prior to admission. No documentation of restriction being communicated or accepted. High risk of non-adherence given prior lifestyle.

resolution_options.option: Explicit driving restriction conversation with patient and wife before discharge, with written documentation
resolution_options.trade_off: Essential for safety and legal compliance. May cause patient distress given loss of independence.

resolution_options.option: Defer driving clearance decision to neurology at 2-week follow-up without explicit prohibition at discharge
resolution_options.trade_off: Unacceptable — patient may drive in the interim, creating safety and liability risk.

conflicts.summary: Atorvastatin 80 mg + Amlodipine CYP3A4 interaction creates elevated myopathy risk at maximum statin dose

positions.position: Amlodipine moderately inhibits CYP3A4, increasing atorvastatin exposure. At 80 mg (maximum dose), myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk is elevated above baseline. Requires active surveillance.
High-intensity statin (80 mg) is guideline-recommended for secondary stroke prevention with LDL 142. Dose reduction would compromise efficacy.

resolution_options.option: Maintain current regimen with active myopathy surveillance — baseline CK, patient education on symptoms, recheck CK and LFTs at 4–6 weeks
resolution_options.trade_off: Preserves guideline-adherent secondary prevention while managing interaction risk through monitoring. Acceptable approach.

resolution_options.option: Switch to rosuvastatin (not a CYP3A4 substrate) at equivalent high-intensity dose
resolution_options.trade_off: Eliminates interaction but introduces a medication change at discharge; rosuvastatin formulary status unknown.

gaps.summary: Prolonged cardiac monitoring for paroxysmal AFib not ordered or documented as planned
gaps.source: Physician agent identifies that admission ECG showed normal sinus rhythm but no extended cardiac monitoring (30-day event monitor or Holter) is documented. In cryptogenic stroke with PFO, undetected paroxysmal AFib would change management from antiplatelet to anticoagulation.
gaps.action_required: Order 30-day cardiac event monitor before discharge or confirm neurology/cardiology will arrange at first outpatient visit. Document plan explicitly.

gaps.summary: Home health PT/OT eligibility under Medicare Advantage home health benefit not assessed
gaps.source: MSW agent identifies that homebound criteria eligibility has not been evaluated. Patient ambulates only 50 feet with SPC and is unsteady on uneven surfaces — may meet homebound definition. Home health PT/OT is a separate benefit from outpatient PT and would not count against the 20-visit annual cap.
gaps.action_required: Contact Medicare Advantage plan immediately to verify home health eligibility criteria. If eligible, place home health orders before discharge to maximize available rehabilitation.

gaps.summary: Formal standardized balance assessment not documented — fall risk score is unquantified
gaps.source: PT agent notes that Berg Balance Scale or Tinetti POMA has not been administered. Fall risk is rated MODERATE-HIGH based on clinical impression but lacks standardized baseline score for tracking recovery and substantiating medical necessity in any insurance appeals.
gaps.action_required: Administer Berg Balance Scale or Tinetti POMA before discharge. Document score. Essential for PT baseline and insurance appeal documentation.

gaps.summary: Complete OTC and supplement medication history not obtained
gaps.source: Pharmacist agent notes only lisinopril 10 mg documented as home medication. No assessment of OTC medications (especially NSAIDs, aspirin, fish oil, vitamin E) or herbal supplements that could interact with DAPT.
gaps.action_required: Complete home medication review including OTC and supplements at discharge counseling. Specifically ask about ibuprofen/naproxen use, fish oil, vitamin E, ginkgo, and any herbal products.

gaps.summary: Baseline CK and LFTs not documented prior to high-intensity statin initiation
gaps.source: Pharmacist agent notes atorvastatin 80 mg started for secondary prevention but no baseline CK or hepatic function documented. Given amlodipine-atorvastatin CYP3A4 interaction, baseline values are important for monitoring.
gaps.action_required: Obtain baseline CK and LFTs before discharge or document plan for outpatient baseline within 1–2 weeks.

gaps.source: MSW agent notes no IPV or elder abuse screening documentation in available data. Absence of documentation is not absence of risk.
gaps.action_required: Complete universal domestic safety screen per institutional protocol. Document outcome.

gaps.summary: Stair negotiation and outdoor environment not assessed despite patient's prior active outdoor lifestyle
gaps.source: PT agent notes stair negotiation was not assessed, and home entry (outdoor steps, driveway, walkway surfaces) is unknown. Patient previously walked 2 miles daily outdoors.
gaps.action_required: Clarify whether home entry has exterior steps. If yes, stair training required before discharge or barrier must be mitigated. Outdoor surface types should be assessed.

prioritized_actions.action: Complete goals-of-care conversation with patient and wife regarding IRF refusal. Document informed refusal with specific language about functional consequences: 20-visit PT cap, left arm recovery trajectory, fall risk, and 18–22% 30-day readmission risk. Clarify IRF is not a nursing home. Keep IRF authorization pathway available if patient reconsiders.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Discharge cannot proceed without documented informed refusal. Current refusal based on 'not a nursing home' may be modifiable with education. Patient autonomy must be respected but requires full information.

prioritized_actions.action: Communicate driving restriction explicitly to patient and wife. Document in chart, discharge instructions, and discharge summary. Patient must not drive until cleared by neurology (3–6 months post-stroke per MA guidelines).
prioritized_actions.rationale: Patient is a former driver with left hemiparesis, proprioceptive deficits, and dysmetria. Driving before clearance creates unacceptable risk to patient and public. Legal and safety imperative.

prioritized_actions.action: Complete teach-back with patient AND wife on Clopidogrel stop date (2026-05-06). Provide written calendar reminder. Dispense exactly 21 tablets. Confirm PCP has received explicit stop date notification.
prioritized_actions.rationale: DAPT course is 21 days per POINT trial protocol. Continuation beyond this date increases bleeding risk without additional benefit. Early discontinuation increases stroke risk. Date must survive discharge transition.

prioritized_actions.action: Counsel patient explicitly that NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, etc.) are absolutely contraindicated during DAPT course. Document in discharge instructions. Acetaminophen is acceptable alternative.
prioritized_actions.rationale: NSAIDs with concurrent DAPT markedly increase GI and intracranial hemorrhage risk. Patient may use OTC NSAIDs for pain without realizing danger.

prioritized_actions.action: Assess Medicare Advantage home health PT/OT eligibility. Determine if patient meets homebound criteria. If eligible, place home health orders to supplement inadequate 20-visit outpatient cap.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Home health benefit is separate from outpatient PT and does not count against 20-visit cap. This is potentially the most significant unclaimed resource for rehabilitation adequacy.

prioritized_actions.action: Order 30-day cardiac event monitor for paroxysmal AFib evaluation in cryptogenic stroke workup. If AFib detected, anticoagulation would be indicated over current antiplatelet regimen.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Current DAPT regimen may be suboptimal if paroxysmal AFib is ultimately identified. PFO presence makes cryptogenic workup essential.

prioritized_actions.action: Confirm DME delivery and installation (grab bars, shower chair, handheld shower attachment) before discharge. Verify equipment is in home, not just ordered.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Patient requires standby assist for bathing with moderate-high fall risk. Bathroom without safety equipment is a fall hazard.

prioritized_actions.action: Complete structured caregiver education with Patricia Jackson: bathing assist technique, fall response protocol, left arm positioning/passive ROM, medication schedule, stroke warning signs (FAST), when to call 911 vs. PCP.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Wife is sole caregiver for patient with moderate functional deficits. Untrained caregiver increases patient fall risk and caregiver injury risk.

prioritized_actions.action: Verify Clopidogrel formulary tier and copay under patient's specific Medicare Advantage HMO plan. If cost barrier identified, explore manufacturer assistance or GoodRx.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Non-adherence to antiplatelet therapy due to cost is a known, preventable cause of early stroke recurrence.

prioritized_actions.action: Obtain repeat BMP (creatinine, potassium, eGFR) before discharge to confirm stable renal function following lisinopril dose doubling. If not obtained before discharge, explicitly order for PCP 1-week visit.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Lisinopril doubled from 10 mg to 20 mg. Risk of undetected hyperkalemia or AKI. Metformin safety also depends on eGFR remaining adequate.

prioritized_actions.action: Administer Berg Balance Scale or Tinetti POMA before discharge. Document baseline score.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Standardized score essential for tracking recovery trajectory and substantiating medical necessity in insurance appeals for additional PT visits.

prioritized_actions.action: Complete stroke warning sign education (FAST/BE-FAST) with patient and wife. Teach-back required. Emphasize: call 911, do not drive to hospital.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Stroke recurrence risk is highest in first 30 days post-stroke. Delayed recognition and delayed care worsen outcomes.

prioritized_actions.action: Confirm outpatient PT/OT referral is placed with in-network provider under Medicare Advantage HMO. Confirm first appointment date is in hand before discharge, not just 'pending scheduling.'
prioritized_actions.rationale: Every day without therapy in the neuroplasticity window increases risk of permanent functional deficit. 'Referral placed' is not sufficient — appointment date required.

prioritized_actions.action: Counsel patient on myopathy warning signs (muscle pain, weakness, dark urine) given atorvastatin 80 mg plus amlodipine CYP3A4 interaction. Obtain baseline CK if not done. Schedule CK and LFT recheck at 4–6 weeks.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Elevated myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk at maximum statin dose with CYP3A4 inhibitor co-administration. Patient has no prior statin experience — no baseline for symptoms.

prioritized_actions.action: Instruct patient to discard or sequester old Lisinopril 10 mg tablets at home. Dose is now 20 mg. Risk of taking old supply at incorrect dose.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Medication dose change with old supply at home creates dosing error risk.

prioritized_actions.action: Complete home medication review including OTC medications and supplements. Specifically ask about NSAIDs, fish oil, vitamin E, ginkgo, and herbal products.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Unrecognized OTC anticoagulants or NSAIDs interact with DAPT.

prioritized_actions.action: Confirm PCP appointment within 1 week of discharge with explicit agenda: Clopidogrel stop date review, BMP recheck, medication reconciliation, HbA1c follow-up plan, BP monitoring.
prioritized_actions.rationale: LACE 10 requires follow-up within 48–72 hours to 1 week. Multiple medication changes require early PCP review.

prioritized_actions.action: Complete domestic safety screening per institutional protocol. Document outcome.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Universal precaution. Absence of documentation is not absence of risk.

prioritized_actions.action: Educate patient and wife on post-stroke emotional lability as a neurological symptom. Provide written guidance and stroke support resources (American Stroke Association).
prioritized_actions.rationale: Mild emotional lability documented. Wife needs to understand this is neurological, not volitional, to prevent relationship strain and misattribution.

prioritized_actions.action: Flag HbA1c 8.1% for PCP and/or endocrinology at follow-up. Document need for diabetes management optimization.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Suboptimal glycemic control increases cerebrovascular and microvascular risk. Not a discharge barrier but requires explicit outpatient action plan.

prioritized_actions.action: Clarify home entry for exterior steps. If present, stair training required before discharge or alternate entry must be identified.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Patient is unsafe on uneven surfaces. Unassessed exterior steps are a fall hazard on every entry/exit from home.

prioritized_actions.action: Place caregiver support referral (Family Caregiver Alliance or local equivalent) and stroke survivor peer support referral (American Stroke Association).
prioritized_actions.rationale: Patricia Jackson has moderate burnout risk as sole full-time caregiver with no IRF buffer. Proactive support reduces caregiver crisis risk.

prioritized_actions.action: Assess patient's ability to open medication bottles with left hand grip 3/5. Order easy-open caps if needed. Recommend pill organizer.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Child-resistant caps may be difficult with left hand limitation. Small barrier but affects daily medication adherence.

prioritized_actions.action: Schedule fasting lipid panel recheck at 6–8 weeks post-discharge to assess atorvastatin response. LDL target <70 mg/dL.
prioritized_actions.rationale: Atorvastatin started with LDL 142. Need to confirm response to high-intensity statin. Also triglycerides 198 — persistent elevation may require additional intervention.

handoff_packages.pcp_summary:
Dear Primary Care Colleague,

Robert Jackson (DOB: 1961-09-03, MRN: MRN-502847) is a 65-year-old male discharged on [DATE] following an 8-day hospitalization for acute right MCA territory ischemic stroke (I63.411).

**Admission Course:** Presented emergently with NIHSS 6, BP 196/108. MRI confirmed right MCA infarct (cortical/subcortical, ~18 mL, no hemorrhagic transformation). TTE revealed EF 58% and small PFO without resting shunt. Carotid Doppler showed no significant stenosis. NIHSS improved to 2 at discharge with residual left arm weakness (grip 3/5) and mild dysmetria. BP now controlled at 138/82 on expanded antihypertensive regimen.

**Discharge Medications:**
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily (continue indefinitely)
- Clopidogrel 75 mg PO daily — **STOP DATE: 2026-05-06** (21-day DAPT per POINT trial, then aspirin monotherapy)
- Atorvastatin 80 mg PO daily (NEW — high-intensity statin for secondary prevention, LDL 142 at admission)
- Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily (NEW — for BP control)
- Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily (INCREASED from 10 mg home dose)
- Metformin 1000 mg PO BID (held during acute phase, restarted Day 6)

**Key Labs:** Cr 1.0, eGFR 78, K+ 4.3 (2026-04-22). Glucose 138. HbA1c 8.1% — above target, requires optimization. LDL 142, HDL 38, TG 198.

**LACE Score: 10 (HIGH)** — 30-day readmission risk 18–22%. Follow-up within 1 week is critical.

**Active Problems Requiring Follow-Up:**
1. Clopidogrel stop date 2026-05-06 — please confirm patient discontinues and continues aspirin monotherapy
2. BMP recheck — lisinopril dose doubled; confirm Cr and K+ stable
3. HbA1c 8.1% — diabetes management optimization needed
4. Atorvastatin + amlodipine CYP3A4 interaction — monitor for myopathy; CK/LFTs at 4–6 weeks
5. PFO identified — cardiology follow-up in 4 weeks for closure discussion
6. Driving restriction — patient must not drive until cleared by neurology (typically 3–6 months post-stroke in MA)
7. Outpatient PT/OT limited to 20 visits under Medicare Advantage — patient refused IRF against recommendation

**Unresolved Issues:** 30-day cardiac monitoring for paroxysmal AFib evaluation not yet arranged — please confirm with neurology/cardiology. Home health PT eligibility under separate Medicare benefit should be explored.

**Follow-Up Appointments:** PCP within 1 week (you), Neurology 2 weeks, Cardiology 4 weeks, PT/OT ASAP.

Please contact [Hospital] at [Phone] with questions. Thank you for ongoing care of this patient.

Requires standby assist for bathing, assist for lower body dressing. Independent feeding, grooming (limited left hand use), toileting. NIHSS 2.

complete_medication_list: Aspirin 81 mg PO daily — continue indefinitely for stroke prevention
Clopidogrel 75 mg PO daily — STOP on 2026-05-06 (21 days total), then aspirin alone continues
Atorvastatin 80 mg PO daily — NEW, take at same time each day, indefinite
Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily — NEW, for blood pressure
Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily — INCREASED from 10 mg (discard old 10 mg tablets)

interactions_flagged: Aspirin + Clopidogrel: Intentional DAPT for 21 days. Increased bleeding risk — patient should report black/tarry stool, unusual bruising, prolonged bleeding, severe headache.

Amlodipine + Atorvastatin: Amlodipine increases atorvastatin levels (CYP3A4 interaction). At 80 mg atorvastatin, myopathy risk is elevated. Patient must report unexplained muscle pain, weakness, or dark urine immediately.
NSAID CONTRAINDICATION: Patient must NOT take ibuprofen, naproxen, or any NSAID while on DAPT — dramatically increases GI and intracranial hemorrhage risk. Acetaminophen is safe alternative.
new_medications_requiring_counseling: Atorvastatin 80 mg — This is a cholesterol-lowering medication to prevent another stroke. Take once daily at the same time. Do NOT stop without talking to your doctor. Report muscle pain, weakness, or dark urine immediately. Avoid grapefruit in large amounts.
Amlodipine 10 mg — This is a blood pressure medication. Ankle swelling is a common side effect — report if bothersome but not dangerous. Take at the same time daily.
Aspirin 81 mg — This thins your blood to prevent clots. Continue indefinitely. Take with food if stomach upset occurs.

Clopidogrel 75 mg — This also thins your blood. **You will take this for exactly 21 days only — your last dose is on May 6, 2026.** After that date, STOP clopidogrel but CONTINUE aspirin. Do not stop early. Do not continue past May 6 unless your doctor tells you to.
cost_and_access_issues: Clopidogrel copay under Medicare Advantage HMO should be verified — if cost is a barrier, GoodRx or manufacturer discount can reduce 21-day supply to $10–15
All other medications are generic and low-cost ($4–10/month at most retail pharmacies)
Dispense exactly 21 tablets of Clopidogrel — not a 30-day supply
adherence_risk_factors: 4 of 6 medications are new — significant new medication burden
Clopidogrel has hard stop date that must be tracked
Left hand grip 3/5 may make bottle opening difficult — assess for easy-open caps
High health literacy and engaged wife are protective factors
counseling_priorities: 1. Clopidogrel stop date 2026-05-06 — this is the single most important medication counseling point
2. NSAID avoidance — no ibuprofen, no naproxen, no Aleve, no Advil, no Motrin
3. Statin myopathy monitoring — atorvastatin 80 mg with amlodipine increases risk
4. Lisinopril dose change — take 20 mg, discard old 10 mg tablets
5. Pill organizer recommended — wife can help with weekly setup

handoff_packages.patient_instructions:
**Why You Were in the Hospital**
You had a stroke. Part of your brain did not get enough blood, which caused weakness on your left side. You are now stable and ready to continue your recovery at home.

**Your Medications**
You are going home with 6 medications. The most important things to know:

1. **Clopidogrel (the small pink pill)** — You will take this for exactly 21 days. **Your last dose is on Tuesday, May 6, 2026.** After that, STOP this pill. Keep taking your aspirin.

2. **Aspirin** — Take this every day for the rest of your life. It helps prevent another stroke.

3. **Atorvastatin** — This lowers your cholesterol. If you get unexplained muscle pain or dark urine, call your doctor right away.

4. **DO NOT take ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin) or naproxen (Aleve).** These pills can cause dangerous bleeding when taken with your blood thinners. Tylenol is safe.

**Danger Signs — Call 911 Immediately**
- Face drooping on one side
- Arm weakness (especially new or worse)
- Trouble speaking or understanding words
- Sudden severe headache
- Sudden vision changes

**Do not drive yourself to the hospital. Call 911.**

**Driving**
You cannot drive until your neurologist clears you. This is usually 3–6 months after a stroke. Patricia will drive you to all appointments.

**Your Appointments**
- Primary care doctor: within 1 week
- Neurologist: 2 weeks
- Cardiologist: 4 weeks
- Physical therapy: as soon as possible — call to schedule if you don't have a date

**Questions?** Call your doctor's office or the hospital at [Phone].

meta.confidence_notes: Confidence is moderate due to several unresolved elements: (1) Goals-of-care conversation has not yet occurred — discharge readiness depends on its outcome; (2) Home health PT eligibility under Medicare Advantage has not been assessed — this is the single largest opportunity to address the rehabilitation adequacy gap; (3) 30-day cardiac monitoring for paroxysmal AFib is a workup gap identified by physician but not addressed in any agent output as resolved; (4) Baseline CK and LFTs not documented for statin monitoring; (5) Formal standardized balance assessment not completed. All five specialist agents provided comprehensive outputs with appropriate clinical grounding. Conflicts and gaps identified are actionable and must be resolved before discharge can proceed safely.

functional_mobility.ambulation_distance: 50 feet with single-point cane (SPC) on level surfaces; unsteady and unsafe on uneven surfaces. Significant gap from baseline of 2-mile independent daily walks.
functional_mobility.assistive_device: Single-point cane (SPC) — new this admission; appropriate for current level but may require upgrade to quad cane or hemi-walker if left leg weakness progresses or fatigue worsens.
functional_mobility.gait_quality: Asymmetric gait pattern with left hemiparetic deviation. Reduced left ankle dorsiflexion (4-/5) creates foot-clearance risk. Left leg proprioception deficits noted on exam contribute to instability. Does not safely navigate uneven terrain.
functional_mobility.endurance: Markedly reduced from baseline. 50-foot ambulation distance represents approximately 2.5% of prior daily walking capacity. Cardiovascular deconditioning after 8-day acute admission compounded by neurologic deficits.
functional_mobility.baseline_prior_admission: Fully independent community ambulator — walked 2 miles daily, no assistive device, no mobility limitations documented.

Emerging left arm spasticity reducing protective extension reflex on left side

Reduced endurance — fatigue-related gait deterioration likely beyond 50 feet
Obesity (BMI 32.4) — increases fall consequence severity and complicates recovery from near-falls

Mild post-stroke emotional lability — attention and impulsivity risk during ambulation

rehab_recommendation.justification: Robert Jackson meets IRF admission criteria on all clinical dimensions: (1) Right MCA territory ischemic infarct with documented mild left hemiparesis, emerging spasticity, and gait deficits — a condition for which IRF outcomes data are substantially superior to home PT at this functional deficit level; (2) Cognition intact — oriented x4, no aphasia, follows complex commands, NIHSS cognitive subscale normal — he will participate meaningfully in 3+ hours of daily therapy; (3) Motivation and engagement are high; (4) Qualifying 3-day inpatient stay met; (5) Insurance coverage exists (20 days IRF). The functional gap is severe: patient walked 2 miles daily pre-stroke and currently cannot safely ambulate beyond 50 feet, cannot manage uneven surfaces, has left arm strength 3/5 with emerging spasticity, and requires standby assist for bathing and lower-extremity dressing. Without intensive interdisciplinary rehab in the critical early neuroplasticity window (first 30–90 days post-stroke), permanent functional deficit in the left upper extremity is the expected trajectory. The 20-visit outpatient cap represents a clinically inadequate dose for an MCA stroke at this severity — IRF followed by home PT represents the optimal care pathway. Patient refusal of IRF is documented and creates a critical unresolved conflict that must be escalated to the full team.
rehab_recommendation.frequency_per_week: If home PT elected against recommendation: 5 visits per week minimum in weeks 1–3, tapering to 3 per week weeks 4–6 — but 20-visit annual cap makes this impossible to execute within standard of care.
rehab_recommendation.estimated_duration_weeks: IRF: 2–3 weeks acute inpatient followed by transition to home PT or outpatient. If home PT only: clinically indicated dose is 8–12 weeks; available dose under current coverage is approximately 4–6 weeks at 3x/week before cap is reached.

Flooring surface types unknown — throw rugs and transitions between flooring types are high fall-risk for hemiparetic gait
Bedroom-to-bathroom distance not documented — overnight fall risk if far from toilet
Outdoor surface access unknown — driveway, walkway, and entry steps not assessed; patient previously active outdoors
Left-hand grip 3/5 with emerging spasticity — door handles, faucets, and stair railings may require modification

Given fall risk level, in-person home evaluation strongly preferred if home discharge proceeds.

physician.clinical_summary: Robert Jackson is a 65-year-old male admitted emergently on 2026-04-15 with an acute right MCA territory ischemic infarct (NIHSS 6 on admission, estimated volume 18 mL), presenting with left hemiparesis in the setting of severely uncontrolled hypertension (196/108 on arrival) and previously unoptimized vascular risk factors including LDL 142, HbA1c 8.1%, and obesity. Workup identified a small PFO without resting shunt and carotid arteries without significant stenosis. A three-way conflict between clinical team recommendation for IRF, patient's firm refusal of any inpatient facility, and a restrictive 20-visit outpatient PT cap under Medicare Advantage HMO represents the primary unresolved discharge barrier.

drug_interactions.clinical_implication: Intentional DAPT for secondary stroke prevention per POINT trial protocol. Significantly increased bleeding risk compared to monotherapy — GI bleed, intracranial hemorrhage. Benefit window is 21 days; after 2026-05-06 risk-benefit favors monotherapy.
drug_interactions.recommended_action: This combination is intentional and guideline-supported for the defined 21-day course. Reinforce absolute clopidogrel stop date of 2026-05-06 with patient, wife, and PCP in writing. Counsel patient on bleeding warning signs (black/tarry stool, unusual bruising, prolonged bleeding from cuts, severe headache). Avoid NSAIDs and additional antiplatelet agents during DAPT course.

drug_interactions.mechanism: Both are CYP3A4 substrates. Atorvastatin may marginally compete for CYP3A4-mediated metabolism of clopidogrel's active metabolite conversion; clinical significance at standard doses is considered low.
drug_interactions.clinical_implication: Theoretical minor reduction in clopidogrel antiplatelet effect. At standard doses of both agents, clinical impact is not considered significant. Not a reason to alter therapy.
drug_interactions.recommended_action: No action required. Flag for awareness only. Monitor clinical response.

drug_interactions.mechanism: ACE inhibitor-induced renal hemodynamic changes can transiently reduce GFR; if eGFR drops, metformin accumulation risk increases.
drug_interactions.clinical_implication: With recent lisinopril dose doubling, renal function should be re-checked within 1–2 weeks of discharge. If eGFR falls below threshold, metformin dose or safety may need reassessment.
drug_interactions.recommended_action: Ensure BMP (creatinine/eGFR) is checked at PCP visit within 1 week of discharge. Flag PCP to reassess metformin safety if eGFR declines.

drug_interactions.mechanism: High-dose aspirin can attenuate the antihypertensive and renoprotective effects of ACE inhibitors via prostaglandin pathway inhibition. At 81 mg/day, this interaction is considered clinically minimal.
drug_interactions.clinical_implication: At 81 mg aspirin dose, interaction is not clinically significant. BP currently well controlled at 138/82.
drug_interactions.recommended_action: No action required at current aspirin dose. Continue BP monitoring at follow-up visits.

drug_interactions.mechanism: Amlodipine inhibits CYP3A4, the primary enzyme responsible for atorvastatin metabolism. This can increase atorvastatin plasma concentrations and potentially myopathy risk.
drug_interactions.clinical_implication: Atorvastatin 80 mg is already the maximum recommended dose and is associated with the highest myopathy risk among statins. Concurrent amlodipine increases exposure further. Prescribing labeling recommends atorvastatin dose not exceed 20 mg when combined with some strong CYP3A4 inhibitors; amlodipine is a weak-to-moderate inhibitor, and 80 mg atorvastatin is used in this combination clinically, but requires active myopathy surveillance.
drug_interactions.recommended_action: Prescriber should be aware of this interaction. Counsel patient on myopathy/rhabdomyolysis symptoms: unexplained muscle pain, weakness, dark (cola-colored) urine. Obtain baseline CK if not already done. Monitor CK and LFTs within 4–6 weeks of discharge. Do not add additional CYP3A4 inhibitors (azithromycin, diltiazem, verapamil, grapefruit) without reassessment.

dose_adjustment_flags.detail: Current eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m² (CKD-EPI, 2026-04-22). Metformin 1000 mg BID is within acceptable range for eGFR ≥60. However, eGFR was drawn prior to lisinopril dose doubling (lisinopril increased Day 2; eGFR drawn Day 7). Post-discharge eGFR may shift. Additionally, patient is a 65-year-old Black male — race-free CKD-EPI equation should be confirmed as the estimation method used. If eGFR falls to 45–59, dose reduction should be considered; if eGFR falls below 30, metformin must be discontinued.
dose_adjustment_flags.action_required: Recheck BMP including creatinine/eGFR at PCP follow-up (1 week). Confirm race-free CKD-EPI equation used for eGFR estimation. PCP to reassess metformin dosing if eGFR declines post-lisinopril up-titration.

dose_adjustment_flags.detail: Dose doubled from 10 mg to 20 mg during hospitalization. Most recent creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, eGFR 78. An acceptable rise in creatinine up to 30% from baseline is expected and tolerable with ACE inhibitor initiation/up-titration. However, this has not yet been rechecked following dose increase. Potassium 4.3 mEq/L currently normal; hyperkalemia risk increases with ACE inhibitor dose escalation, particularly if eGFR declines.
dose_adjustment_flags.action_required: BMP (creatinine, potassium, eGFR) must be rechecked at 1-week PCP visit. If creatinine rises >30% from baseline or potassium exceeds 5.5 mEq/L, prescriber should reassess lisinopril dose.

dose_adjustment_flags.detail: Atorvastatin 80 mg is guideline-recommended high-intensity statin for secondary stroke prevention in a 65-year-old. No dose adjustment needed for age alone. However, given amlodipine co-administration (CYP3A4 interaction, see drug interactions), myopathy risk is elevated above expected baseline for high-intensity statin therapy. Patient is not on any renally or hepatically cleared medications requiring adjustment, but hepatic function has not been documented in available data.
dose_adjustment_flags.action_required: Confirm LFTs at baseline or within 4–6 weeks of statin initiation. Counsel patient on myopathy symptoms. No dose change indicated at this time, but prescriber should be notified of amlodipine-atorvastatin CYP3A4 interaction.

contraindications.contraindication: Iodinated contrast exposure during acute admission
contraindications.reason: Metformin was appropriately held during acute admission. Record indicates it was restarted Day 6. Per standard guidance, metformin should be held for 48 hours after iodinated contrast administration and restarted only after renal function is confirmed stable. The restart on Day 6 appears compliant with this protocol given contrast was likely administered at or near admission. eGFR on Day 7 is 78 — acceptable. This is flagged for documentation completeness, not as an active contraindication at discharge.
contraindications.action_required: Confirm contrast-to-restart interval was ≥48 hours and eGFR was checked prior to Day 6 restart. Document in discharge summary. PCP should recheck eGFR at 1-week visit as follow-up.

contraindications.contraindication: Concurrent DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) with active stroke history
contraindications.reason: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, etc.) taken concurrently with DAPT markedly increase GI and intracranial hemorrhage risk. Patient is a 65-year-old male post-stroke on dual antiplatelet therapy. Any NSAID use — including OTC — is contraindicated during the DAPT course and should be avoided. Patient must be counseled explicitly.
contraindications.action_required: Counsel patient explicitly that ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin), naproxen (Aleve), and all NSAIDs are contraindicated during DAPT course. Acetaminophen is acceptable alternative for pain/fever. Document in discharge instructions. Flag for PCP.

factors: New medications: 4 of 6 discharge medications are new — significant new medication burden requiring patient education

Clopidogrel time-limited course with hard stop date (2026-05-06) — missed stop dates are common and clinically dangerous (continued DAPT beyond 21 days increases bleeding risk without additional benefit)
Twice-daily metformin dosing adds complexity relative to all other once-daily medications
Mild left hemiparesis may affect ability to open pill bottles — assess dexterity for medication handling
Atorvastatin 80 mg is a new medication for a patient with no prior statin experience — myopathy side effects may lead to self-discontinuation without adequate counseling
Post-stroke emotional lability documented — may affect follow-through on complex medication instructions in early recovery period
High health literacy is a protective factor — patient researches conditions, asks questions, is engaged
Wife is present, retired, and committed — strong caregiver support is a major protective factor
Medicare Advantage coverage in place — most medications expected to be covered, reducing cost-related non-adherence risk

access_affordability.detail: Medicare Advantage HMO formulary tier for generic clopidogrel varies by plan. Generic clopidogrel is widely available but some Medicare Advantage plans tier it at a cost-sharing level that may create a copay burden for a 21-day supply. Case record notes 'may have copay — verify.'
solutions: Verify formulary tier and copay with patient's specific Medicare Advantage HMO prior to discharge
If cost barrier identified, GoodRx or manufacturer discount programs for generic clopidogrel can bring cost to $10–15 for 21-day supply at most retail pharmacies
Ensure 30-day supply is NOT dispensed — patient only needs 21-day supply. Dispense exactly 21 tablets to avoid confusion about stop date and unnecessary cost.

access_affordability.detail: Generic atorvastatin 80 mg is widely available and low cost ($4–10/month at most pharmacies). Medicare Advantage formulary coverage for generic statins is near-universal. Cost concern is low.
solutions: Generic atorvastatin available at major retail chains for $4–10/month
Confirm formulary coverage at discharge — essentially all Medicare Part D plans cover generic atorvastatin

access_affordability.detail: Generic amlodipine is extremely low cost and on virtually all Medicare formularies. No significant access concern. Flagged only to note it is a new medication requiring dispensing at discharge.
solutions: Generic amlodipine available for $4/month or less at most retail pharmacies

access_affordability.detail: Patient's Medicare Advantage HMO is a retired municipal worker plan. Confirm that all 6 discharge medications are covered under the specific plan's Part D formulary. If any require prior authorization (uncommon for this medication list but possible under strict HMO formularies), the process should be initiated before discharge to prevent gaps in therapy.
solutions: Perform formulary check for all 6 medications against patient's specific plan prior to discharge
Send prescriptions electronically to patient's preferred pharmacy prior to discharge so medications are ready for pickup on discharge day
Confirm patient has transportation to pharmacy (wife drives — adequate per social history)

flags.flag: CRITICAL — NSAID CONTRAINDICATION DURING DAPT: Patient must not take any NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, etc.) while on dual antiplatelet therapy. GI and intracranial hemorrhage risk is significantly elevated. Patient must be explicitly counseled before discharge.
flags.action: Include explicit NSAID prohibition in discharge instructions. Counsel patient and wife verbally and in writing. Flag for PCP and any prescribing providers. Acetaminophen is acceptable alternative.

flags.flag: CRITICAL — CLOPIDOGREL HARD STOP DATE 2026-05-06: DAPT course is 21 days per POINT trial protocol. Clopidogrel must be discontinued on 2026-05-06. Continuation beyond this date does not provide additional stroke prevention benefit and substantially increases bleeding risk. PCP and patient must both have this date documented.
flags.action: Document stop date prominently in discharge summary, medication reconciliation list, and patient-facing discharge instructions. Dispense exactly 21 tablets of clopidogrel. PCP follow-up at 1 week should confirm patient understands and plans to stop. Consider calendar reminder or pill count strategy with patient/wife.

flags.flag: HIGH — AMLODIPINE + ATORVASTATIN CYP3A4 INTERACTION: Amlodipine moderately inhibits CYP3A4-mediated atorvastatin metabolism, increasing atorvastatin exposure at an already-maximum 80 mg dose. Myopathy/rhabdomyolysis risk is elevated above baseline for high-intensity statin therapy.
flags.action: Counsel patient on myopathy warning signs (unexplained muscle pain, weakness, dark urine). Prescriber should be notified of interaction. Obtain baseline CK if not done. Recheck CK and LFTs at 4–6 week follow-up. Avoid additional CYP3A4 inhibitors.

flags.flag: HIGH — RENAL FUNCTION RECHECK REQUIRED POST-DISCHARGE: Lisinopril was doubled during admission. eGFR most recently 78 (Day 7) but drawn before full hemodynamic effect of dose change. Metformin safety depends on eGFR remaining ≥45. Risk of hyperkalemia with ACE inhibitor up-titration.
flags.action: BMP (Na, K, Cr, eGFR) must be checked at PCP visit within 1 week of discharge. PCP to reassess both metformin and lisinopril dosing based on results. Flag in PCP communication.

flags.flag: HIGH — METFORMIN HELD FOR CONTRAST: Metformin was appropriately held during acute phase and restarted Day 6. Confirm contrast-to-restart interval ≥48 hours and that eGFR was checked prior to restart. Document in discharge summary.
flags.action: Review inpatient medication administration record to confirm compliant restart timing. Document in discharge summary. PCP to recheck eGFR at 1-week visit.

flags.flag: MEDIUM — HbA1c 8.1%: Diabetes management suboptimal. Metformin monotherapy appears to be current regimen. Post-stroke, optimal glycemic control is important for cerebrovascular risk reduction, but aggressive hypoglycemia must also be avoided (hypoglycemia is stroke mimic and increases injury risk).
flags.action: Flag for PCP and/or endocrinology at follow-up to reassess diabetes management regimen. Do not adjust diabetes medications at hospital discharge without outpatient follow-up plan in place. Ensure patient is aware of hypoglycemia risk, especially post-stroke.

flags.flag: MEDIUM — PILL BURDEN AND NEW MEDICATION EDUCATION: Patient is being discharged on 6 medications, 4 of which are new. Twice-daily metformin is the only non-once-daily medication. Patient has high health literacy and strong caregiver support, but formal medication counseling at discharge is essential, particularly for clopidogrel stop date, atorvastatin myopathy monitoring, and NSAID avoidance.
flags.action: Pharmacist-led medication counseling session prior to discharge. Provide written medication list with indications, timing, and stop dates. Use teach-back to confirm understanding. Provide wife with copy of medication list.

flags.flag: MEDIUM — OTC AND SUPPLEMENT RECONCILIATION INCOMPLETE: No home medications were documented as active prior to lisinopril 10 mg (only home medication on record). Patient is a 65-year-old male — confirm whether any OTC medications (especially NSAIDs, aspirin, fish oil, vitamin E, herbal supplements) were being taken prior to admission. These may interact with DAPT.
flags.action: Perform complete home medication review at discharge counseling including OTC and supplements. Document in discharge summary. Specifically ask about ibuprofen/naproxen use, fish oil, vitamin E, and any herbal blood thinners.

flags.flag: LOW — TRIGLYCERIDES 198 mg/dL: Mildly elevated. High-intensity atorvastatin will provide some triglyceride-lowering benefit. No additional agent indicated at this time. Dietary counseling relevant given obesity (BMI 32.4) and T2DM.
flags.action: Recheck fasting lipid panel at 6–8 week follow-up after atorvastatin initiation. If triglycerides persist above 200 mg/dL despite statin, PCP/cardiology to consider additional intervention at outpatient follow-up.

pharmacist.confidence_note: Confidence is moderate rather than high for the following reasons: (1) Complete home medication list prior to admission is not fully documented — only lisinopril 10 mg is listed as a home medication; unrecognized OTC medications or supplements that interact with DAPT cannot be excluded. (2) Exact eGFR estimation methodology (race-free vs. race-based CKD-EPI) is not confirmed in the record — relevant for a Black male patient where historical race-based equations overestimate eGFR. (3) Baseline CK and hepatic function (LFTs/AST/ALT) are not documented in the available labs, limiting full atorvastatin safety assessment. (4) Timing of contrast administration relative to metformin restart on Day 6 cannot be fully verified from available data alone. All drug interaction and pharmacological data referenced reflects established clinical evidence.

2nd alternative: Short-term skilled nursing facility (SNF) with intensive PT/OT — lower intensity than IRF but more structured than home; may be acceptable middle-ground if patient is unwilling to consider IRF but open to a facility other than a 'nursing home.' Coverage under Medicare Advantage should be verified.

3rd alternative: Home with home health PT/OT under Medicare Advantage home health benefit — may provide additional visits beyond outpatient cap if homebound criteria are met. Eligibility should be assessed immediately, as current ambulatory status (50 feet with SPC, unsteady on uneven surfaces) may qualify.

msw.confidence_note: Social context is reasonably well-documented for a hospital case: living situation, caregiver, insurance type, SDOH flags, and functional status are present. Confidence is moderate rather than high for the following reasons: (1) Medicare Advantage HMO benefit details (home health eligibility, exact copay structure, in-network rehab options) are not fully specified in the source data — these gaps directly affect the discharge plan viability; (2) Domestic safety screening is undocumented — absence of documentation is not absence of risk; (3) Caregiver assessment is based on family meeting attendance and wife's stated commitment, not a structured caregiver capacity or burden assessment; (4) Patient's stated reason for refusing IRF ('not going to a nursing home') has not been fully explored — the depth of this preference and whether it is modifiable with education is unknown. These gaps are actionable, not just informational.
